title: LinearB 2026 benchmarks: AI-generated PRs accepted at 32.7% vs 84.4% for humans
summary: LinearB's 2026 Software Engineering Benchmarks Report, drawing on 8.1M+ pull requests from 4,800+ organizations, finds AI-generated PRs are accepted at 32.7% versus 84.4% for human-authored PRs, and wait 4.6x longer for first review but are reviewed 2x faster once picked up. Acceptance rates diverge sharply by tool — Devin trending up since April, Copilot slipping since May. The metric exposes a hidden cost of agentic coding: review queues are absorbing the productivity gains, and reviewer cognitive load is becoming the new bottleneck. The data is among the first large-N evidence that bot-PR economics differ structurally from human-PR economics.
whyItMatters: A 51-point acceptance gap means agent throughput claims must be discounted by rework and abandoned-PR volume — vendor ROI math typically ignores this.
Review queues becoming the constraint flips the optimization target: invest in reviewer tooling and triage policy, not faster code generation.
Tool-level variance (Devin up, Copilot down) suggests AI tool selection is now an ongoing optimization, not a one-time procurement.
recommendedActions: Track bot-PR acceptance rate and time-to-first-review as first-class metrics alongside DORA.
Set policy on bot-PR labeling, reviewer routing, and automated pre-review checks before scaling agent volume.
Run periodic vendor bake-offs on your own acceptance-rate data, not vendor benchmarks.
risksAndCaveats: LinearB's customer base skews toward larger, instrumented orgs — small teams may see different ratios.
'Acceptance rate' definitions vary across teams (merged, closed-without-merge, rewritten); cross-tool comparisons need caveats.
It is not entirely clear when the data for the study were collected. The oldest survey data is from 2024, developer usage survey from November 2025, and Engineering leadership survey 2026.
